title: Japan and India Must Harness Middle-Power Momentum in the Indo-Pacific
gist: The 16th India-Japan summit, marking PM Sanae Takaichi's first visit to India, advanced AI, defense, energy and supply-chain cooperation and signals that middle powers like Japan and India are building bilateral buffers as Chinese economic coercion intensifies and US strategic consistency wavers.

insights.claim: As China's economic coercion intensifies and Washington's strategic consistency wavers, middle powers are increasingly turning to one another to preserve autonomy and build strategic buffers.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Watch for deepening bilateral and minilateral ties among non-superpower Indo-Pacific states (Japan-India, and similar pairings) as a hedge trade independent of US-China dynamics.
insights.quote: As Beijing's economic coercion intensifies and Washington's strategic consistency wavers, middle powers are increasingly looking to one another to preserve their autonomy and build buffers.
insights.locator: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's timely first visit to India

insights.claim: The Japan-India relationship could become one of the most consequential partnerships in redefining the rules of the Indo-Pacific order.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: A strengthening Tokyo-Delhi axis is a structural theme for Indo-Pacific security and supply-chain positioning, not a one-off diplomatic event.
insights.locator: Tokyo and Delhi are at the forefront of this trend

insights.claim: PM Sanae Takaichi's first visit to India, for the 16th India-Japan summit, produced cooperation commitments across AI, defense, energy and supply-chain security.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Concrete cooperation areas (AI, defense, energy, supply chains) mark where bilateral deals and capital flows are likely to follow.
insights.locator: the summit advanced cooperation across AI, defense, energy and supply chain security

themes.theme: Historical foundations of the India-Japan bond

insights.claim: Japan and India's partnership benefits from the absence of World War II-era grievances, since Japan never occupied India during its expansionist conquest of Asia, unlike its history with other regional states.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: The lack of historical baggage removes a friction point that complicates Japan's other regional relationships, making faster trust-building and deeper cooperation plausible.
insights.locator: the two countries are not dogged by World War II baggage

insights.claim: Indian jurist Radhabinod Pal was the sole judge at the International Military Tribunal for the Far East to vote to acquit all 25 Japanese defendants, an act some conservative Japanese consider a cornerstone of the India-Japan relationship.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Explains a durable, elite-level goodwill reservoir in Japan toward India that helps sustain bilateral ties across political cycles.
insights.locator: Indian jurist Radhabinod Pal voted alone to acquit all 25 defendants

insights.claim: India and Japan's relationship is anchored by deep cultural ties rooted in Japan's embrace of Buddhism, which spread from India in the sixth century.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Long-standing cultural affinity provides a soft-power foundation that reinforces the durability of the strategic partnership.
insights.locator: rooted in deep cultural ties, particularly Japan's embrace of Buddhism
